questions.prompt: Select all that are true about a single trie node.
choices: It stores a map from a single character to a child node.
It stores a boolean end-of-word flag.
The end-of-word flag marks that a complete stored word ends at this node.
Walking through it costs one constant-time child lookup per character step.
questions.explanation: A node holds exactly a per-character child map and a boolean end-of-word flag that marks a complete word, and each step of a walk is a single constant-time child lookup.

questions.prompt: Which statements about a trie are true?
choices: Words that share a prefix share the same path until they diverge
search, insert, and startsWith are each O(length of the string)
The time cost grows with the number of words stored
search must check the end-of-word flag, but startsWith does not
questions.explanation: Cost is O(length), independent of how many words are stored. The flag distinguishes search from startsWith.

questions.prompt: If ONLY "card" has been inserted, which of these return true? (If none, select none.)
questions.explanation: None. Car and ca are unflagged prefixes, and cardable walks off the end of the stored path.

questions.prompt: Order the steps of inserting a word into a trie.
items: Start at the root (the empty prefix)
For each character, follow its child, creating an empty node if missing
Step into that child and continue
After the last character, set the end-of-word flag on the final node
correct_order: Start at the root (the empty prefix)
For each character, follow its child, creating an empty node if missing
Step into that child and continue
After the last character, set the end-of-word flag on the final node

questions.prompt: "Given a fixed dictionary of words and many queries, each asking whether a string is a stored word or whether any word starts with a given prefix." Which pattern(s) apply?
questions.explanation: Prefix queries against a fixed dictionary are the trie fingerprint. A hash set is a reasonable secondary for the exact-membership half but cannot do prefixes.

questions.prompt: Explain why a trie needs an explicit end-of-word flag, using an example where a stored word is a prefix of another stored word.
questions.actual_answer: Walking a path in a trie only proves that those characters exist as a prefix of some stored word. It does not prove that the prefix is itself a word. Suppose you insert both "app" and "apple". The path a-p-p exists because it is on the way to "apple", but "app" is also a real stored word, and the only way to record that fact is a boolean flag on the second p node. Without the flag, search would either report every prefix as a word. Wrongly matching "ap". Or have no way to tell that "app" is stored while "ap" is not. The end-of-word flag is exactly the bit that separates "a word ends here" from "this node is only passed through."
questions.rubric: Full credit: a path existing only proves a prefix exists, not a word; the flag records which nodes are real word-ends; concrete example where one word is a prefix of another (app/apple) and both facts must be distinguishable. Partial: states the flag's purpose without the prefix-of-another example. Low: vague.
